## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; BBAAX exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.
  
  **Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.
  
  Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.
  
  ## Cost
  
  ~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.
  
  ## Scope
  
  - Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
  - Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
  - Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
  - Triage routing per surprise:
    - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
    - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
  - Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions
  
  ## Constraints
  
  - Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
  - **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift
  - Re-runs within the pace are first-class; each re-run draws fresh quota
  - Per BBAAN's invest-fatal-on-exists and divest-deletes-production-RBRA: any retry path must respect cult-verb posture; reset between attempts goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery
  - Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue
  
  ## Cross-pace dependencies
  
  - gauntlet-suite-assembly landed (rbw-tP and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist)
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Restoring RBRAs — gauntlet self-mints all role credentials (BBAAN's invest cult-verb); only Payor OAuth is the operator's prerequisite
  - /rbk-prep-release integration (BBAAH)
  - Multi-day re-run scenarios
  - Cleanup beyond what rbw-tP itself does — operator peeks post-success and runs cleanup tabtargets manually if desired

Encode site (line 480) becomes 'openssl enc -base64 -A < BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE' replacing 'base64 < BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE'; the two heredoc decode sites (line 497 in the b|w branch and line 507 in the c branch) become 'echo .. | openssl enc -base64 -d -A > ztmp' replacing 'echo .. | base64 -d > ztmp'. Function recap: garrison step5 plants the workload SSH privkey on the remote via an admin SSH heredoc; raw PEM cannot be embedded inline (multi-line, cross-shell quoting drift), so the local side base64-flattens it to a single-line [A-Za-z0-9+/=] blob, embeds it in the heredoc, and the remote decodes back to byte-exact PEM into a tmp file before install/cp to the target path with workload ownership and 0600 mode. The -A flag is load-bearing: it suppresses openssl's default 76-column line-wrap on encode (so the heredoc-embedded blob is a single line) and tolerates absence of newlines on decode (so single-line input works). Existing line-486 newline-strip (z_key_b64 //$'\n'/) becomes belt-and-suspenders rather than load-bearing under -A; left in place per docket scope (no incidental cleanup).   ## Character
  
  First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; BBAAX exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.
  
  **Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.
  
  Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.
  
  ## Cost
  
  ~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.

  - Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
  - Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
  - Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
  - Triage routing per surprise:
    - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
    - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
  - Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions
  
  ## Constraints
  
  - Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
  - **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift

  - Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue
  
  ## Cross-pace dependencies
  
  - gauntlet-suite-assembly landed (rbw-tP and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist)
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Restoring RBRAs — gauntlet self-mints all role credentials (BBAAN's invest cult-verb); only Payor OAuth is the operator's prerequisite
  - /rbk-prep-release integration (BBAAH)
  - Multi-day re-run scenarios
  - Cleanup beyond what rbw-tP itself does — operator peeks post-success and runs cleanup tabtargets manually if desired

content.text:
## Character

Validate BBAAX's repairs (charge auto-summon + ordain rmi removals) by re-running rbw-tP end-to-end on a branch named after this pace's coronet. Branch isolation lets parallel attempts (e.g., simultaneous macOS + Linux runs) avoid the commit-trail race on main.

## Wrap criterion

Green tally end-to-end. Same guardrail as BBAAX — partial completion is not pass.

## Cost

~90m wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt.

## Scope

- Create a branch named after this pace's coronet, off current main
- Run rbw-tP from marshal-zero on that branch
- Operator selects which machine the pace mounts on (mac or linux); pace does NOT use foray
- Triage surprises per BBAAX rules: tiny inline fix + re-run, larger → follow-up pace

## Locked decisions

- Branch name == pace coronet (identity-stable; no human-meaningful name)
- Branch lives until pace wraps; deletion is operator territory

aiTitle: Investigate parallel test conflicts between Linux and macOS

content.text:
Found it: **₢BBABA "rbw-tP-branched-rerun"**. The docket explicitly cites "simultaneous macOS + Linux runs" as a use case enabled by branch isolation — but the isolation it solves is the git commit-trail race on `main`. The user's concern is right: that's not the only collision surface.

## What collides if mac + linux run in parallel against the same payor manor

**Branch isolation handles**: rbrr.env mutations (each fixture rewrites RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER and commits — pristine-lifecycle and canonical-establish both do this; per-coronet branches keep those commits on separate refs).

**Branch isolation does NOT handle** — this is where the user's concern is correct:

1. **Depot-moniker TOCTOU on the shared manor.** Both fixtures use a walk-depot-list-and-pick-next-free allocator (`rbtdrk_pick_next_moniker` for canonical with stem `canest2`, `rbtdrp_pick_next_moniker` for pristine-lifecycle). It's not atomic. Two runs walk concurrently, both see N as free, both try to create project N — second `gcloud projects create` fails. This is the most likely failure.

2. **Pristine-lifecycle's throwaway prefixes are hard-coded constants** — `RBTDRP_THROWAWAY_CLOUD_PREFIX = "prlc-"` and `RBTDRP_THROWAWAY_RUNTIME_PREFIX = "prlr-"`. Both runs install identical prefixes. The cloud-side moniker walker still discriminates by depot_list (so still just the TOCTOU above), but any side-state keyed on cloud_prefix alone (GAR, GCS bucket naming) is shared across the two parallel pristine runs.

3. **Governor SA on the manor is singleton.** Recent BBAAN landed "invest-fatal-on-exists" cult-verb posture. If both runs hit governor-mantle / invest-director / invest-retriever near-concurrently, one wins and the other gets a fatal-on-exists. (Mantle itself may be idempotent — I didn't verify — but the SA-invest paths the gauntlet exercises are not.)

4. **Runtime side (RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX) is NOT a parallel-run hazard** — `canr-tadmor` etc. compose docker project names, but docker daemons are per-host. Mac and linux have separate container/network namespaces. Only matters if both targeted the same docker daemon (they don't).

## Recommendation

The docket's "simultaneous mac + linux" framing is overstated for the current fixture design. Two paths forward:

- **Tighten the docket**: declare BBABA explicitly *serial* (operator picks one machine), and slate a separate follow-up pace if parallel runs are actually wanted — that pace would need to address the TOCTOU and SA singletonness (e.g., reservation-by-creation, hostname/PID suffix in moniker, or skip-on-exists for governor/director/retriever).
- **Or keep the parallel framing** but acknowledge in the docket that two runs are racing on depot-moniker allocation and shared-manor SAs, and the operator owns serializing those phases manually.

I'd take the first — drop the parallel-runs claim from the locked decisions, since it advertises a capability the fixture allocators don't actually have. Want me to reslate?

message.content: I'm musing we could add a BURS element which is a 'station discriminator prefix' that could be added to everything 'global' in the tests like the cloud prefix, the runtime prefix, and the canest thing- would that work?

content.text: Let me check how Governor/Director/Retriever SAs are named, since that's the most load-bearing question for whether a station prefix actually segregates everything.

Yes, it works — with one important caveat and a couple of gotchas.

## Why it works

The collision surfaces are all *names* on the shared payor manor. Prepending a per-station prefix to the name-generation inputs partitions the namespace cleanly:

- **`RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX`** → `${BURS_DISCRIMINATOR}-canc-` → different GAR repos, GCS buckets, depot project IDs.
- **`canest2` family stem** → `${BURS_DISCRIMINATOR}-canest2` → each station walks its own family in `depot_list`. The TOCTOU is unreachable because the racing sets are disjoint.
- **`prlc-` / `prlr-` throwaway prefixes** in `rbtdrp_pristine.rs` (currently hard-coded constants) → station-prefixed → pristine-lifecycle runs no longer share names either.

## The caveat

**SA names live in a different code path than rbrr.env.** Currently `rbgg_Governor.sh:544,595` composes `${RBCC_role_retriever}-${z_identity}` and `${RBCC_role_director}-${z_identity}`. The Governor SA itself is similar. If the station prefix only reaches into rbrr.env-derived names, both stations still fight over `director-alice` on the shared manor — fatal under BBAAN's invest-fatal-on-exists posture.

So the rule has to be: **station prefix applies to every name visible at the manor scope**, including SA short-names. That's the load-bearing requirement; without it, you've solved 80% of the collisions and the SA paths still race.

## Other things to think through

- **Layering shift.** Today RBRR is repo-config (committed, identical across checkouts of this repo). BURS is station-config (per-machine). Composing them means the *effective* cloud prefix is now station-dependent — two checkouts no longer see the same resources. Deliberate, not accidental, but worth being explicit about in RBSRS/RBSRR.
- **Length budget.** GCP project ID is capped at 30 chars. Today's compose uses ~20. A 2–3 char `BURS_DISCRIMINATOR` is fine, but the validator at `rbrr_regime.sh:109` needs to incorporate the new term.
- **Migration.** Existing depots/SAs/GAR repos all live under non-station-prefixed names; they orphan once this lands. One cleanup hit.
- **Runtime prefix is genuinely redundant for this** — local containers don't collide across daemons. But uniform application keeps the rule "every global name carries the station discriminator" simple, and the bytes are free.

## Default-value question

What does an unset `BURS_DISCRIMINATOR` mean? Empty string (current behavior, parallel runs explicitly opt-in) is safest. Hostname-derived auto-default is convenient but turns a station-config field into a "magic" computed value, and BURS values today are explicit (BURS_USER, BURS_LOG_DIR).

## Verdict

The shape is right and BURS is the correct tier for it. If you want this as a permanent capability rather than a one-time workaround for BBABA, slate it as a follow-up with the SA-name reach as part of scope — not just rbrr.env. For BBABA itself, the cheaper move is still "operator runs serially" and reslate BBABA's docket to drop the parallel-run claim.

content.text:
Yes — that meaningfully simplifies the design and removes most of my earlier concerns. Test-only scope means no production-code changes and no migration of existing resources.

## What works cleanly with test-only scope

The test fixtures already control all the inputs to the global namespaces:

- **Pristine throwaway prefixes** (`prlc-`/`prlr-` in `rbtdrp_pristine.rs:65-66`) — replace the hardcoded constants with values composed from `BURS_DISCRIMINATOR`. Test-only.
- **Canonical family stem** (`canest2` in `rbtdrk_canonical.rs:61`) — same: compose from BURS.
- **rbrr.env writes** — fixture installs `${BURS_DISCRIMINATOR}canc-` etc. The production code reading rbrr.env doesn't care; it just sees a different prefix.
- **Director/Retriever SA names** — `rbgg_Governor.sh:544,595` builds `${role}-${identity}` from a parameter the test passes. Fixture passes `${BURS_DISCRIMINATOR}-alice` instead of `alice`. **No production code change** — the discriminator rides into the name through the existing identity slot.

For all four, the production code stays vanilla. The discriminator is purely a test-side composition.

## The one residual collision: Governor mantle

The Governor SA is singleton-per-manor by design — there's no identity parameter on `rbw-aM`. The canonical fixture (`rbtdrk_governor_mantle_impl`) does mantle the Governor as part of its run, and the heat paddock confirms "gauntlet self-mints all role credentials." If both stations target the same payor manor and both mantle in parallel, they're racing on the same SA regardless of any test-side prefixing.

Three ways out, in increasing operator-friction:

1. **Mantle is already idempotent enough.** If `rbw-aM` already does probe-then-mantle (no-op if Governor SA exists with key), parallel mantles converge. Worth confirming before committing to a design.
2. **Skip-mantle option in the fixture.** Add a fixture-config flag that says "Governor exists, just load the key" — operator runs mantle once outside the fixture, then both stations skip it. Test-only change.
3. **Separate payor manor per station.** Cleanest semantic isolation, but doubles OAuth setup ceremony and quota.

If (1) holds, the BURS-discriminator approach scoped to tests is essentially complete. If not, (2) is the right pairing — small fixture-side flag plus the discriminator covers everything.

## Verdict on the test-only framing

It's the right cut. Production stays unchanged, no orphaned resources, no schema layering question, and the discriminator does real work exactly where the collisions are. I'd slate it as a follow-up to BBABA — the parallel-runs claim in BBABA's docket gets dropped now, and the new pace lands the discriminator + fixture wiring + the Governor-mantle question explicitly.

Want me to check whether `rbw-aM` is idempotent before going further?

content.text:
You're right. I was wrong about Governor SA location.

`rbgd_DepotConstants.sh:53`: `RBGD_SA_EMAIL_FULL="${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}.${RBGC_SA_EMAIL_DOMAIN}"` — the SA email is keyed to the **depot project**, not the payor manor. So the Governor SA actually lives inside the depot project, alongside Director/Retriever/Mason SAs.

If each station creates its own depot (which the BURS-discriminator-on-cloud-prefix gives you for free, since the depot project ID composes from cloud_prefix + moniker), then every SA — Governor included — is provisioned inside that station's per-station GCP project. Two parallel runs touch different projects entirely. No singleton conflict.

So the picture collapses cleanly: **station discriminator on the test-side composition of cloud_prefix and family stems → disjoint depot projects → disjoint everything inside them, including Governor.** The Director/Retriever identity-prefixing I mentioned earlier is even redundant once depots are separate, though it's harmless.

The only thing left to think about is what the manor does host — OAuth user, billing account, Cloud Build quota, possibly some IAM bindings between the OAuth principal and the depot. Those are shared, but they're either rate-limit concerns (not collisions) or one-time bindings that idempotent operations handle fine. None of them name-collide.

So: design works, scope is small (rbrr.env-write composition + the two hardcoded fixture constants), no production touch, no Governor concerns. Worth slating.
